title_en: Chapter on the Ruling on Sorcery
content_en:
Sorcery (sihr) occupies a significant place in Islamic jurisprudence and theology because it represents one of the gravest violations of Tawhid — it is among the seven destructive sins (al-sab' al-mubiqat) explicitly enumerated by the Prophet ﷺ, and its practice almost universally involves invoking other than Allah or drawing upon demonic assistance. This chapter in Kitab al-Tawhid establishes the Islamic position on sorcery clearly and without ambiguity.

The foundational Quranic text on this subject is the passage in Surah al-Baqarah describing the two angels Harut and Marut, who were sent as a trial and who taught people sorcery: 'And they follow what the devils recited during the reign of Solomon. It was not Solomon who disbelieved, but the devils disbelieved, teaching people magic and that which was revealed to the two angels at Babylon, Harut and Marut. But they do not teach anyone unless they say: We are a trial, so do not disbelieve' (Quran 2:102). This verse is instructive in its explicit characterization of sorcery as kufr (disbelief), confirming that practicing it or seeking it out constitutes a rejection of the boundaries established by Allah.

The classical scholars have defined sihr in various ways, but the most comprehensive definition encompasses any act that produces an effect — whether real or illusory — through the assistance of demons, satanic forces, or unknown spiritual means, typically involving the invocation of or submission to those forces. This is what distinguishes sihr from permissible practices like medicine and natural remedies: sihr operates through channels that fundamentally compromise Tawhid by attributing power and agency to other than Allah or by seeking assistance from beings whose obedience to Allah has been severed.

Among the most frequently cited hadiths in this chapter is the report that the Prophet ﷺ listed sorcery among the seven destructive sins: 'Avoid the seven destructive sins.' The Companions asked: 'O Messenger of Allah, what are they?' He replied: 'Associating partners with Allah, sorcery, killing a soul which Allah has forbidden except by right, consuming usury, consuming the wealth of orphans, fleeing on the day of battle, and slandering chaste, innocent believing women.' (Al-Bukhari and Muslim.) The placement of sorcery immediately after shirk in this list reflects its gravity in Islamic law.

Ibn Abd al-Wahhab also discusses the legal ruling on the practitioner of sorcery (sahir). The majority opinion in the Hanbali school, and a view within other schools as well, holds that the sahir who commits kufr through their practice is to be executed, based on a narration from Umar ibn al-Khattab and practice attributed to Hafsa (may Allah be pleased with them both). This is a matter of significant juristic complexity, and scholars have distinguished between the sahir who commits clear kufr and the practitioner of impermissible but non-kufr practices.

The chapter also addresses the matter of magic worked against the Prophet ﷺ himself — a narration recorded in al-Bukhari in which a spell was cast upon him by Labid ibn al-A'sam, causing him to imagine he had done things he had not done. This narration is used to establish both the reality of sihr as a phenomenon and the superior protection of revelation (the Quran and authentic du'a) as the prescribed remedy. The Prophet ﷺ was cured through revelation, not through counter-magic, establishing the Sunnah of using Quran and authenticated supplications as the legitimate response to sorcery.
